body_txt: Continuity Lock-Out occurs when it becomes impossible for someone to get into a series or franchise in the middle, because the story to that point has become so thick and convoluted that a newcomer will have very little chance of understanding the significance of anything. Whether or not this is acceptable practice is a matter of debate. On the one hand, you want a story to be accessible to people without them having to dedicate a lot of time to consuming it from start to finish. On the other hand, you want to reward viewers for sticking with the work and allow them to continue enjoying the story they were into all along. Indeed, this is one of the main bones of contention between creators and executives — executives want every installment to bring in a new audience, whereas creators generally want to entertain the audience they already have . This is therefore one of the few problems that the creator forces on the executive. In many respects, it really depends on the medium: There are a number of ways to try and prevent Continuity Lock-Out: Compare Lost in Medias Res .
